title: Filipino Culture Knowledge Graph
design_principles: Prioritize regional specificity over pan-Filipino generalization.
Distinguish Bisayan, Ilocano, Kapampangan, Bicolano, and Bangsamoro/Mindanao perspectives from Tagalog-centric framings.
Represent diaspora experience (Filipino-American, OFW, balikbayan) as distinct contexts, not deviations.
Exclude or explicitly mark colonial-era deficit framings of Filipino traits.
Exclude Orientalist or essentialist characterizations that flatten cultural complexity.
Prefer scholarly ethnographic and cultural studies sources over travel writing or pop-anthropology.
Keep romanticized glosses as aliases or caution notes rather than primary definitions.
Preserve stable canonical slugs for cross-referencing and KG integration.
explicit_exclusions_from_primary_nodes: colonial-era characterizations of Filipino passivity or fatalism as inherent traits
untextured 'Filipino hospitality' as a simple personality attribute
hiya as equivalent to Western shame without relational context
utang na loob as simple debt or obligation without reciprocity dynamics
bahala na as pure fatalism without its active acceptance component
pan-Filipino generalizations that treat Tagalog norms as universal

domains.label: Core Filipino values and ethics
domains.description: Foundational relational and ethical values that structure Filipino social life: kapwa, loob, hiya, utang na loob, bahala na, amor propio, and related constructs.

domains.label: Family and kinship structures
domains.description: Extended family (pamilya), compadrazgo/godparent system (ninong/ninang), fictive kin, balikbayan dynamics, OFW family separation, and filial obligation patterns.

domains.label: Social harmony and conflict dynamics
domains.description: Pakikisama, SIR (smooth interpersonal relations), indirect communication, conflict avoidance, face-saving, and how disagreement surfaces in Filipino social contexts.

domains.label: Language and communication patterns
domains.description: Filipino/Tagalog, regional languages (Cebuano, Waray, Hiligaynon, Ilocano, Kapampangan, etc.), Taglish code-switching, honorifics (po/opo), and language as identity marker.

domains.label: Regional and ethnic cultures
domains.description: Bisayan/Visayan culture (Cebuano, Waray, Hiligaynon), Ilocano, Kapampangan, Bicolano, Maranao/Bangsamoro, Igorot/Cordilleran — distinct norms, values, and identities within the Philippines.

domains.label: Religion and folk spirituality
domains.description: Filipino folk Catholicism, syncretic practices, anting-anting (talismans), folk healing (albularyo), Santacruzan, patron saint devotion, fiesta as religious practice, and folk belief systems.

domains.label: Colonial legacy and historical consciousness
domains.description: Spanish colonial impact (language, religion, social hierarchy), American colonial legacy (education system, English, pop culture), Japanese occupation trauma, and how colonial history shapes present-day identity and institutions.

domains.label: Filipino-American and diaspora identity
domains.description: 1st generation, 1.5 generation (1G), and 2nd generation dynamics; FOB vs. Fil-Am tension; balikbayan identity; OFW experience; identity negotiation across cultures; 'not Filipino enough' phenomenon.

domains.label: Food, fiesta, and material culture
domains.description: Food as relational practice (kain na), fiesta culture, regional cuisines (Bisayan, Ilocano, etc.), the balikbayan box as material expression of diaspora care, and gift-giving norms.

domains.label: Gender, sexuality, and family roles
domains.description: Gender roles in Filipino family life, bakla identity (distinct from Western gay identity), transgender in Filipino cultural context, the babaylan tradition, contemporary LGBTQ+ dynamics, and intersections with Catholicism.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A relational moral-affective regulator tied to propriety, social face, and accountability to others, not equivalent to purely intrapsychic Western shame.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded concept for first-pass operational coverage; refine with page-level citations and regional examples in next pass.
populate_fields.scholarly_notes: Frequently mistranslated as mere shyness or shame; context and relationship matter.

citations.title: From Colonial to Liberation Psychology
citations.why_it_was_used: Core framing of hiya within indigenous Filipino social psychology.

citations.title: Filipino Cultural Traits
citations.why_it_was_used: Discusses common debates and misreadings of hiya in social life.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A relational ethical orientation in which selfhood is understood through shared personhood with others (hindi ibang-tao / hindi-iba-sa-akin), making social obligation and mutual recognition foundational rather than optional.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded concept for first-pass operational coverage; refine with page-level citations and regional examples in next pass.
populate_fields.scholarly_notes: Should be treated as a family of relational practices with regional inflections, not a static trait.

citations.title: From Colonial to Liberation Psychology
citations.why_it_was_used: Foundational articulation of kapwa in Sikolohiyang Pilipino.

citations.authors: K. M. Pe-Pua and Elizabeth Protacio-Marcelino
citations.title: Sikolohiyang Pilipino (Filipino Psychology): A Legacy of Virgilio G. Enriquez
citations.why_it_was_used: Widely used synthesis of Filipino indigenous psychology concepts.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A durable reciprocal debt of gratitude embedded in social relations and moral memory, exceeding transactional debt and often shaping long-term loyalty, assistance, and deference expectations.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded concept for first-pass operational coverage; refine with page-level citations and regional examples in next pass.
populate_fields.scholarly_notes: Should be modeled as reciprocal moral economy, not one-way patronage by default.

citations.title: Social Acceptance Reconsidered
citations.why_it_was_used: Classic treatment of reciprocity and social acceptance dynamics in the Philippines.

citations.title: From Colonial to Liberation Psychology
citations.why_it_was_used: Situates utang na loob within Filipino relational ethics rather than economics.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A practice of maintaining social cohesion through accommodation, tact, and cooperative adjustment, often balancing personal preference against group harmony.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded concept for first-pass operational coverage; refine with page-level citations and regional examples in next pass.
populate_fields.scholarly_notes: Can support cooperation or silence dissent depending on power asymmetry.

citations.title: Social Acceptance Reconsidered
citations.why_it_was_used: Seminal framing of social acceptance and pakikisama in low-conflict interaction styles.

citations.title: Pakikisama: A Philippine value in social interaction
citations.why_it_was_used: Focused treatment of pakikisama as social process.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A cluster of interactional practices (e.g., pakikisama, euphemism, indirect repair, face-work) that reduce overt interpersonal friction and preserve workable social ties in Filipino settings.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded concept for first-pass operational coverage; refine with page-level citations and regional examples in next pass.
populate_fields.scholarly_notes: Useful analytic lens but should not erase conflict, coercion, or strategic noncompliance.

citations.title: Social Acceptance Reconsidered
citations.why_it_was_used: Canonical source for SIR as an analytic construct in Philippine social interaction.

citations.title: Reciprocity in the Lowland Philippines
citations.why_it_was_used: Supports relational and reciprocity mechanisms often grouped under SIR dynamics.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A modern ethnopolitical identity connecting diverse Moro peoples of Mindanao and the Sulu archipelago through shared histories of Islam, anti-colonial resistance, and self-determination, while retaining internal ethnic and class differences.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded from scholarly monographs/articles plus select community sources; expand with full page-level citations in next pass.

citations.title: Muslim Rulers and Rebels
citations.why_it_was_used: Core ethnographic and political history of Moro politics.

citations.title: Preamble and foundational provisions
citations.why_it_was_used: Authoritative basis for contemporary Bangsamoro political identity.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A meta-ethnolinguistic formation spanning Cebuano, Hiligaynon, Waray, and related groups across the Visayas and much of Mindanao, where identity is shaped by multilingualism, migration, and regional literary/public cultures rather than a single unified language or tradition.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded from scholarly monographs/articles plus select community sources; expand with full page-level citations in next pass.

citations.title: The Bisayan Dialects of the Philippines

populate_fields.definition_dense: An ethnolinguistic identity rooted in the Ilocos region but extended through internal migration and diaspora, including Hawaiʻi, where language maintenance, kin obligation, and heritage education shape contemporary Ilocano self-understanding.
populate_fields.source_quality_notes: Seeded from scholarly monographs/articles plus select community sources; expand with full page-level citations in next pass.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A Central Luzon ethnolinguistic formation marked by strong regional self-consciousness and deep links to colonial and national politics, alongside ongoing language-shift pressures and revitalization efforts.

populate_fields.definition_dense: A category of indigenous ritual specialist, healer, spirit medium, and community authority—especially a Visayan term, though many Philippine societies have different local labels for related roles.

populate_fields.definition_dense: In Moro societies, Islamic practice is historically layered through relations among faith, adat (custom), kinship, local authority, and reformist currents rather than a single uniform orthodoxy.
